coredata.dc:title: Sustainable synthesis of carbon quantum dots from shrimp shell and its emerging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Elango, Duraisamy
Packialakshmi, Jeyakumar Saranya
Manikandan, Velu
Jayanthi, Palaniyappan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are emerging nanomaterials with low-cost manufacturing technologies and highly fluorescent in nature with a wide range of emerging applications. Herein, a waste shrimp shell was utilized for the sustainable synthesis of CQDs via the one-step hydrothermal method. The obtained results showed that the prepared S-CQDs have spherical structures with good fluorescent stability and biocompatibility. Even more, the availability of the C–O–C and C–H functional groups makes it extremely hydrophilic in nature. The synthesized CQDs have quantum yield of about 27.14% against quinine sulfate as a reference standard. Bio-imaging analysis ascertains their intracellular accumulation in nematodes and clearly observable fluorescent imaging for the structure of a model organism. Antibacterial analysis using CQDs reveals good antibacterial activity against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria. Therefore, prepared CQDs are considered as promising bio-imaging and antibacterial agent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Renewable resource
Shrimp shell waste
Bioimaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR spectrum, (b) XRD pattern, (c) TEM image of monodispersed CQDs and (d) SAED pattern (e) UV–vis absorbtion spectra, (f) Fluorescence spectra of the synthesized CQDs.
XPS full survey spectrum of CQDs (a) high-resolution XPS (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s, (e) Bioimaging of in vivo model nematode, (f) Antibacterial activity of synthesized CQDs.
Comparison for the CQDs synthesis source and its application.

head.ce:title: Sustainable synthesis of carbon quantum dots from shrimp shell and its emerging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Science, Periyar University, Salem 636011, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of BioNano Technology, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam-dareo, Sujeong-gu, Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do 131120, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of BioNano Technology, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam-dareo, Sujeong-gu, Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do 131120, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are emerging nanomaterials with low-cost manufacturing technologies and highly fluorescent in nature with a wide range of emerging applications. Herein, a waste shrimp shell was utilized for the sustainable synthesis of CQDs via the one-step hydrothermal method. The obtained results showed that the prepared S-CQDs have spherical structures with good fluorescent stability and biocompatibility. Even more, the availability of the C–O–C and C–H functional groups makes it extremely hydrophilic in nature. The synthesized CQDs have quantum yield of about 27.14% against quinine sulfate as a reference standard. Bio-imaging analysis ascertains their intracellular accumulation in nematodes and clearly observable fluorescent imaging for the structure of a model organism. Antibacterial analysis using CQDs reveals good antibacterial activity against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria. Therefore, prepared CQDs are considered as promising bio-imaging and antibacterial agent.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a novel class of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials. Carbon quantum dots possess unique properties that render them a fortunate platform for biology, energy devices, catalysis, and many emerging applications [1]. CQDs have attracted extensive attention in cellular bioimaging field owing to their astonishing biocompatibility. Hence, great efforts have been focused on the promising amalgamation of eco-friendly carbon-based nanomaterials [2–4]. Green synthesis using natural resources holds great promise for multifunctional applications of CQDs [4]. A lot of natural precursors were utilized for the synthesis of CQDs (Table 1 ) [5–10]. In the present work, shrimp shell waste was utilized to produce CQDs by one-pot hydrothermal method. Increasing consumption of seafood, tons of shrimp shell waste was produced every year. Shrimp shell was considered as a potentially renewable resource and nitrogen-rich feedstock [11]. Recycling and utilizing renewable feedstocks is a sustainable approach that helps to mitigate the environmental impacts of human activity. In this work, we developed a novel high-quality fluorescent carbon quantum dots. The prepared CQDs were explored for bio-imaging and antibacterial application. This study not only promotes the development of CQDs and also provides a new avenue for recycling seafood waste with broad application prospects.

Raw shrimp was purchased from the food market, Salem, Tamilnadu, India. All the chemicals and reagents of analytical grades were used without further purification and the deionized water (DI) was used throughout this study. The highly fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized from waste shrimp cells via the one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, raw shrimp shells were washed and dried and grained at room temperature. About, 5% of this powder was dissolved in deionized water. Subsequently, this mixture was transferred Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and reacted in a hot air oven at 180 °C for 12 h. The resultant solution was filtered and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 20 min. Obtained CQDs were stored at 4 °C. CQDs solutions were taken out and vacuum freeze-dried to acquire the solid form. They were utilized for further applications and physical characterization. The surface morphology and structure of CQD were investigated by HRTEM and X-ray diffraction (XRD) respectively. Optical properties of CQDs were carried out on UV–vis spectrophotometer and fluorescent spectrophotometer respectively. The functional group of CQDs was measured using FTIR Perkin-Elmer instrument (USA). Element and chemical composition of the CQDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The methodology of quantum yield calculation was given as the supporting information. The prepared CQDs were explored for its bioimaging and antibacterial applications.

FTIR spectra of Fig. 1 a reveal the CQDs surface functional groups. The spectrum of CQDs showed the broad peaks 3424 cm−1 and 2963 cm−1 attributed to the existence of the O–H group and methylene or methyl (C–H) functional group due to the presence of the aliphatic hydrocarbons and chitin in shrimp shell [12]. Besides, peaks found at 1630 cm−1 and 1410 cm−1 were due to the existence of CO/CC and C–C functional groups respectively. The presence of strongest peaks in the range of 1115–1000 cm−1 was corresponded to C–O–C and C–O stretching, respectively [5]. The adequate availability of the C–O–C and C–H functional groups on the surface of the shrimp shell-derived CQDs make it highly hydrophilic in nature.

The crystalline structure of the prepared CQDs was characterized by XRD (Fig. 1b). XRD patterns exhibited a wide diffraction peak at 24° due to their lesser size and partial graphitization. In addition, wide peaks were perceived in the XRD patterns, confirming the crystallinity and amorphous nature of the CQDs. HR-TEM (Fig. 1c) confirmed that the CQDs were spherical and ranged from 8 to 10 nm. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 1d) suggests the deprived crystallinity of the prepared CQDs. This observation shows that the CQDs are an amorphous carbon lattice.

Photosensitive properties are a vital factor to decide the fluorescent properties of the CQDs. The Uv–visible absorption spectra (Fig. 1e) of the shrimp shell CQDs in aqueous solution exhibited an intense peak of UV region at ∼265 nm, which corresponds to the n → π transition of the CO band and π → π* transition of the conjugated CC band respectively (Fig. 1f). These findings were spectacularly similar with previously reported studies [13]. Photoluminescence (PL) (Fig. 2 b) of the CQDs was excitation wavelength-dependent, and the highest PL emission peak at 450 nm was observed after excitation at 355 nm. Fluorescence stability of CQDs were analysed for 30 days and it exhibits abundant stability (Fig. S1). The quantum yield of S-CQDs was about 27.14% in comparison with quinine sulfate (QY-0.546 in 0.1 M H2SO4) as a reference standard. The chemical structure and elements of CQDs were further identified by XPS spectra. The high-resolution XPS analysis of C1s, N1s, and O1s was illustrated in (Fig. 2a). As shown in Fig. 2b–d suggest these elements were in the form of oxygenated (CO, C–O), aliphatic or graphitic (CC, C–C), pyridinic, and pyrrolic (C–N) structures respectively [14].

The fluorescent properties of CQDs were extensively used for many biological applications. As compared to conventional organic dyes, CQDs have more stability due to their unique fluorescent property. CQDs have excitation-dependent fluorescence emission, which renders them a potential candidatefor emerging biological applications. Therefore, here we demonstrated the CQDs for in vivo cell imaging of nematodes as a model organism (Fig. 2e). The CQDs localized into the whole body of the nematodes. The bioimaging study of synthesized CQDs had not significantly decreased the nematode viability even at 250 µg mL−1 and it exhibit the photostability, water stability, noble bioimaging properties, and these CQDs were suitable for biocompatibility in biomedical applications. Similarly, CQDs synthesized from banana peel wastes via hydrothermal process showed bioimaging of nematodes with decreased < 5% viability at 200 µg mL-1 and it exhibited the green fluorescence with excellent photostability [14].

The antibacterial activity (Fig. 2f) of prepared CQDs with control streptomycin was carried out against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria. The higher concentration of CQDs (100 μg/mL) showed noteworthy activity against E. coli, K. pneumoniae, B. cereus, and S. aureus. This present study indicates the CQDs from shrimp shells were more effective against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria. The interaction of S-CQDs to the bacteria imposed physical damage on the bacterial cell wall or outer membrane which leads to overall physiological dysfunction and leakage of cytoplasmic components, finally leading to bacterial death [15]. Previously, it has been reported that mushroom-derived CQDs were inhibited by the P. aeruginosa bacterial strains, S. aureus and K. pneumoniae [16].

In summary, carbon quantum dots from waste shrimp shell was magnificently prepared in an ecofriendly manner and exhibited excellent bio-imaging property and antibacterial activity. More prominently, the results of our work may promise to inspire the green, cost-effective and sustainable synthesis of high-quality fluorescent carbon quantum dots. This study demonstrated the shrimp shell CQDs would pave a new avenue to explore the CQDs in emerging applications.

Duraisamy Elango: Conceptualization, Data curation. Jeyakumar Saranya Packialakshmi: Formal analysis, Data curation. Velu Manikandan: Validation, Formal analysis. Palaniyappan Jayanthi: Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by University Grants Commission-NFST Fellowship program (No: 201920-NFST-TAM-00427), India.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2022.131667.
